content: Seat belts still decrease our risk of dying in an accident, but the statistics are not all black and white.
options: Seat belts help to protect passengers from dying in car accidents, but data indicates that the situation is not clear.
Seat belts decrease our risk of dying; that is true.

content: What he found was that contrary to conventional wisdom, mandating the use of seat belts in 18 countries resulted in either no change or actually a net increase in road accident deaths.
options: What he found was that using seat belts in 18 countries resulted in an actual net decrease in road accident deaths.
Surprisingly, he found that enforcing the use of seat belts does not decrease road accident deaths. That is true in 18 countries.

content: Nor will these possible concerns exist in a vacuum.
options: The possible concerns will be affected by other factors.
These possible concerns will exist in outer-space where it's almost a vacuum.

content: The point, stresses Adams, is that drivers who feel safe may actually increase the risk that they pose to other drivers, bicyclists, pedestrians and their own passengers.
options: According to Adams, drivers who feel safe may drive more carelessly, therefore, causing danger to other people.
Adams stresses that drivers who are posers may actually increase their risky behavior endangering other people.

content: In the case of seat belts, instead of a simple, straightforward reduction in deaths, the end result is actually a more complicated redistribution of risk and fatalities.
options: Seat belts reduce the death rate no matter how complicated the distribution of risk and fatalities is.
Seat belts may seem to reduce the death rate, but actually the risks still exist but in a different way.

content: In everyday life, risk is a moving target, not a set number as statistics might suggest.
options: In everyday life, risks varies and is not always quantifiable.
As statistics might suggest, there are risks whenever you move in your everyday life.

content: And still others are fatalists who may believe that a higher power devises mortality schedules that fix a predetermined time when our number is up.
options: Some people believe that lucky numbers may predetermine whether we'll die or not in car accidents.
There are people who believe everything including death is preordained.

content: Consequently, any single measurement assigned to the risk of driving a car is bound to be only the roughest sort of benchmark.
options: Consequently, all measurements used to avoid the risk of driving a car are restricted by rough manufacturing quality.
Therefore, the risk of driving a car cannot be clearly quantified.

content: The bottom line is that risk doesn't exist in a vacuum and that there are a host of factors that come into play, including the rewards of risk, whether they are financial, physical or emotional.
options: Financial, physical and emotional rewards contribute to the increasing risk.
The level of risk has many influences, some of which are positive.

content: We make dozens of risk calculations daily, but you can book odds that most of them are so automatic or visceral—that we barely notice them.
options: Everyday we instinctively and unknowingly make many decisions about our safety.
We calculate risks daily, but we barely notice them if we don't write them down.
